text: The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery, applying hemo-lock clips at its origin, and divides it. A medial to lateral dissection plane is developed in front of Girota's fascia, aided by traction and counter traction. The dissection progresses laterally towards the pancreas, exposing the origin of the enteric vein. The upper border of the pancreas is localized, and the peritoneal layer covering it is carefully divided. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided between clips, avoiding undue traction to prevent bleeding. A swab is placed over the pancreas as an anatomical landmark, completing step one of splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection until reaching the splenocolic attachments, using robotic arms R-II and R-III for traction and counter-traction. An assistant holds the colon to improve exposure. Monopolar diathermy is used to join the medial and lateral dissection layers, while dividing the omental attachments. The swab placed at the lateral edge of the pancreas is visible, marking the completion of the second step of splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon exposes the lesser sac and separates the omentum from the transverse colon. Robotic arm R-II is declutched, and the instrument is removed to minimize conflict. Traction is provided by an assistant on the colon, while robotic arm R-III holds the omentum up and toward the spleen. The posterior wall of the stomach is inspected to confirm entry into the lesser sac. Dissection is carried laterally to join previously created layers on the right of the flexure. The swab placed over the pancreas is visible, and the peritoneal layer above it is divided. The left colon is further mobilized medially by pulling the colon down and holding the omentum up.
